ASSESSMENT GUIDE FINS3666 Trading and Market Making Term 1, 2025

Assessment Summary Assessment Task Weighting Due Date* Learning Outcomes 30% Part 1 Week 3 by 9pm Friday CLO 1, CLO2, CLO4. Assessment 1: Individual Assignment Part 2 Week 5 by 9pm Friday This assessment is divided into three parts. Each part will be Part 3 Week 7 by 9pm Friday started by students as an in-class activity. 30% Week 10 by 9pm Friday CLO1, CLO2, CLO3, CLO4. Assessment 2: Group Project This assessment involves working in small teams to apply quantitative analysis and modelling techniques learned in the course to develop and evaluate trading strategies. Assessment 3: Final Exam 40% Exam period CLO1, CLO2, CLO3, CLO4. This assessment consists of 20 short answer questions that cover content from Weeks 5 to 10 of the course. * Due dates are set at Australian Eastern Standard/Daylight Time (AEST/AEDT). If you are located in a different time-zone, you can use the time and date converter . Course Learning Outcomes (CLO) CLO1: Students will demonstrate a solid understanding of quantitative analysis in financial markets, including the different trading strategies employed and the importance of risk management. [PLO 1, 2, 3, 5] CLO2: Students will be able to apply statistical analysis and modelling techniques to derive insights from financial data, enabling them to develop and evaluate trading strategies. [PLO 1, 2, 4, 6] CLO3: Students will develop practical skills in back testing and performance evaluation, including the ability to design, conduct, and interpret back tests to assess the performance of trading strategies. They will also learn how to evaluate and optimise trading strategies based on the results obtained. [PLO 1, 2, 3, 7] CLO4: Students will develop critical thinking skills in assessing market microstructure, analysing order book dynamics, and understanding the impact of market liquidity on trading strategies. [PLO 1, 2, 6] UNSW Business School 1

Assessment Details Turnitin Turnitin is a tool for checking originality and preventing plagiarism. It compares submitted work against other students' submissions, the Internet, and key resources. If required to submit via Turnitin, a link will be available in your Moodle course site. You can submit drafts before the deadline and use the Similarity Report to refine your writing before the final submission. You can find out more information in the Turnitin information site for students . Late Submissions The parameters for late submissions are outlined in the UNSW Assessment Implementation Procedure . For FINS3666, if you submit your assessments after the due date, you will incur penalties for late submission unless you have Special Consideration (see below). Late submission is 5% per day (including weekends), calculated from the marks allocated to that assessment (not your grade). Assessments will not be accepted more than 5 days late. Extensions You are expected to manage your time to meet assessment due dates. If you do require an extension to your assessment, please make a request as early as possible before the due date via the special consideration portal on myUNSW (My Student profile > Special Consideration). You can find more information on Special Consideration and the application process below. Lecturers and tutors do not have the ability to grant extensions. Special Consideration Special consideration is the process for assessing the impact of short-term events beyond your control (exceptional circumstances), on your performance in a specific assessment task. What are circumstances beyond my control? These are exceptional circumstances or situations that may: • Prevent you from completing a course requirement, • Keep you from attending an assessment, • Stop you from submitting an assessment, • Significantly affect your assessment performance. Available here is a list of circumstances that may be beyond your control. This is only a list of examples, and your exact circumstances may not be listed. You can find more detail and the application form on the Special Consideration site, or in the UNSW Special Consideration Application and Assessment Information for Students . Generative AI and Translation Tools You may use AI-based tools to research and prepare before completing your assessments. Standard wordprocessing features such as spelling, grammar checking, and reference generation are allowed. However, functions that generate, paraphrase, or translate text, including for assessment tasks or presentations, are prohibited. Generative AI can assist in researching topics but is prone to inaccuracies and fabrications. Always verify the information it provides with original sources and consider diverse perspectives. Avoid sharing personal or copyrighted content on platforms like ChatGPT, as it operates through OpenAI. There are resources available about how best to use generative AI available here Chat GPT & Generative AI at UNSW . When you have used AI, you should ensure that it is noted, such as in the online workbook in Assessments 2 and 3. As the other written assessments are personal reflections, using Generative AI as part of your research is not necessary and, as stated above, cannot be used in generating, paraphrasing, or translating texts. For more information about AI and assessment, see here Using AI in Assessments . UNSW Business School 2

Please note that your submission will be passed through an AI-generated text detection tool. If your marker has concerns that your answer contains passages of AI-generated text, you may be asked to explain your work. If you are unable to satisfactorily demonstrate your understanding of your submission you may be referred to UNSW Conduct & Integrity Office for investigation for academic misconduct and possible penalties. Acceptable use in background research: • Idea Generation: Use generative AI to brainstorm potential topics or angles for your assessment. • Preliminary understanding: Consult generative AI to get a basic understanding of a topic before diving into primary source research. • Research assistance: Employ generative AI to provide overviews of complex topics, which you will then verify with primary sources (e.g. Scite ) • Diverse perspectives: Use generative AI to identify various perspectives on a topic, ensuring to cross-check for accuracy with reliable sources. Unacceptable use in producing an assessment task: • Content creation: Do not use generative AI to write or draft any parts of your assessment submission. • Paraphrasing: Avoid using generative AI to reword or paraphrase passages for inclusion in your work. • Translation: Do not use generative AI to translate text that will be used in your submission. • Direct editing: Do not use generative AI for editing functions beyond basic spelling and grammar checks provided by standard word processing software. • Reference generation: Do not use generative AI to generate citation formats for inclusion in your submission. Documentation and integrity: • Disclosure: Clearly note any use of generative AI in your research process, as required, such as in the online workbook. • Verification: After using generative AI for research, thoroughly verify the information against original sources and ensure understanding of the material. • Personal reflections: Do not use generative AI at all for personal reflection tasks, as these are based on your own thoughts and experiences. In case of review: • Explanation preparedness: Be prepared to explain your work and demonstrate a thorough understanding of the content and sources of your submission if questioned. • Academic integrity: Maintain the integrity of your work by avoiding the use of generative AI for tasks where it is prohibited and follow all guidelines for proper citation and originality. Following these guidelines will help ensure that generative AI is used as a tool for enhancing your research and learning, rather than replacing your own efforts and understanding in t
